bersicht Der bertragungsneurosen

Hilda Rollman-Branch M.D.

Hilda Rollman-Branch

Hilda Rollman-Branch  

Ein bisher unbekanntes Manuskript von Sigmund Freud. Mit einem Essay von Ilse Grubrich-Simitis. Frankfurt-am-Main: S. Fischer Verlag, 1985, 129 pp., DM 34

[A PHYLOGENETIC FANTASY: OVERVIEW OF THE TRANSFERENCE NEUROSES. Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard Univ. Press, 1987, $17.50.

The German psychoanalyst Ilse Grubrich-Simitis was working on the correspondence between Freud and Ferenczi. During her research in London she discovered an old suitcase. It contained papers Sandor Ferenczi had given to his student Michael Balint, like Ferenczi a Hungarian, who took them with him to England. To her surprise, Grubrich-Simitis found among Ferenczi's papers a manuscript in Freud's handwriting titled: XII. bersicht der bertragungsneurosen (Overview or Review of the Transference Neuroses).1 Freud's letter, written on the back of the manuscript's last page, confirmed that Grubrich-Simitis has unearthed the last of the seven lost papers on metapsychology.2

Not only does Freud call this manuscript Entwurf (draft)

1This and subsequent translations of quotations from Freud's essay are the reviewer's. The English-language edition of this book had not been published at the time this review was written.2The first five well-known published essays are: "Instincts and their Vicissitudes"; "Repression"; "The Unconscious"; "A Metapsychological Supplement to the Theory of Dreams"; and "Mourning and Melancholia."251

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Irving B. Harrison

David Werman M.D.

David Werman

David S. Werman, M.D.
Duke University Medical Center (Box 3812)
Durham, NC 27710

David Werman  

March 25, 1988

I appreciate Dr. Harrison's thoughtful comments differentiating the oceanic experience from awe, but I am puzzled as to why he thinks I should have made such a distinction. It was not only irrelevant to the purpose of my paper, but he might as well have criticized my failure to differentiate the oceanic experience from the other 25 altered stages of consciousness which I mention. The critical point is that, on the evidence of his letter, it would appear that the main thesis of my paper has eluded Harrison.

Of major importance is Harrison's allegation that I "endorse" the oceanic experience, and, through that back door, that I endorse mysticism. That charge makes as little sense as to endorse, or disapprove of, any altered state of consciousness. I attempted to describe the oceanic experience and to explore it from a psychoanalytic perspective. I used the oceanic experience principally to illustrate the complex nature of consciousness. Curiously, Harrison himself grudgingly admits that in the "rare and fortuitous [sic]" instances of Rolland and Russell, the oceanic experience did serve to enhance ego growth.

563

[[RunningHead]]

Trauma and Mastery in Life and Art

By Gilbert J. Rose. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1987, 239 pp., $27.50.

Ellen Spitz Ph.D.

Ellen Spitz

Ellen Handler Spitz, Ph.D.
Larchmont, New York

Ellen Spitz  

Pondering Gilbert J. Rose's evocative book, I gaze through frosty window panes at thickly swirling snowflakes that slowly blanket the world, and Dostoevsky's Russia seems close enough to touch. The experience accords well with the first comment I would like to make about this book—a word of appreciation to its author for the genuine pleasure he affords his reader in conjuring up so vividly the scenes of which he writes—including, among others, those drawn from the oeuvre of the great Russian novelist as well as from his own clinical practice of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. His power of reanimation, his raconteurship, is a genuine gift and contributes in no small way to the quality of the book under review. In instances where authors are endowed with it, such a gift enhances the pages both of literary and art criticism and of clinical case histories. Indeed, though it is rarely stated in quite these terms, a flair for breathing new life into stories is probably an asset (albeit with accompanying dangers) in clinical work as well, since the experience of empathic listening is often conveyed by means of a heightened capacity to reflect back one's understanding in striking imagery.

Having begun here, however, it is necessary to say that the book under review is not principally one of literary criticism, nor is it chiefly concerned with Dostoevsky. Rather, its stated agenda is considerably more global in scope. The author himself describes it as an effort to build upon his earlier work, The Power of Form, by considering the general relations both between art and psychopathology and between creative or esthetic experience and the process of psychoanalytic treatment. To undertake this ambitious task, he has selected "trauma" and "mastery" as organizing notions that, in his view, cut across the domains he seeks to compare and contrast. Such an agenda implies a theoretical text.

839

Andrew Schwartz's Response

Andrew Schwartz M.D.

Andrew Schwartz

Andrew Schwartz, M.D.
1616 18th Street, N.W.
Washington, DC 20009

Andrew Schwartz  

October 4, 1988

Addressing first Dr. Harrison's criticism of my phrase, "shame intense enough to," I would like very respectfully to suggest that in using the words, "with some chagrin," Dr. Harrison also implies that Freud came to view the Project with an affect belonging basically to a general class which one might label "embarrassment/shame." I, in addition, would want to say that I had wished in the passage cited merely to propose that Freud's feeling of "shame" (over, I implicitly agree, the Project's uncertain scientific viability and/or ambitiousness) was "intense enough" to lead him ultimately to abandon the work—and, incidentally, to regard it with considerable ambivalence even during its gestation. In his "Editor's Introduction" to the Project, Strachey (1966; S.E. 1, p. 285) observes, "And on November 29 (Letter 36), he [Freud] wrote: 'I can no longer understand the state of mind in which I hatched out the "Psychology"; I cannot make out how I came to inflict it on you.'" Even allowing for a possibly facetious tone in Freud's letter to Fliess, one can infer that Freud's words do convey a flavor of self-directed scorn which, in turn, arguably reflects the "shame"/"chagrin"-imbued attitude which both Dr. Harrison and I, with different terms, note. In any event, I wish sincerely to add that I did not intend a "gratuitous insult" to Freud: I feel that there is no "shame" in feeling "shame" over the—partial—failure of a very ambitious, complex, if premature synthetic effort. Moreover, as my paper conveys, I believe that there are reasons to regard the Project and its author as having been strikingly prescient.

Changing Psychic Structure Through Treatment: Criteria for Change

Stanley Olinick M.D.

Stanley Olinick

Stanley L. Olinick, M.D.
2819 Ellicott Street, N.W.
Washington, DC 20008

Stanley Olinick  

ABSTRACT

The author outlines his clinical observations during the "middle game" of psychoanalysis, leading to recognition that structural change is taking place. "Middle game," "structure," process, and content are defined and critically discussed. Illustrative clinical vignettes are offered. The presentation emphasizes the importance of an active and resolving transference "struggle"; in addition, more traditionally noted criteria are briefly touched on, e.g., development of observing ego and treatment alliance, changes in dream function and communication, and the reviewing of the neurosis and transference during the termination phase as instances of mourning and working through. The concept of optimal psychobiological function in the service of a homeostatic principle is discussed.

Psychoanalytic Enlightenment and the Greening of Diplomacy

Joseph Montville

Joseph Montville

Joseph V. Montville
8602 Bonnie Drive
Yorktown Village Path
Annandale, VA 22003

Joseph Montville  

ABSTRACT

The author reviews aspects of psychoanalytic theory which have helped him in his career as an American diplomat and political analyst. He cites the works of Erikson (1950), (1968), (1969), Volkan (1979), (1987), and Mack (1979), (1983) as especially helpful, and praises the late Jeanne Knutson, founder of the International Society of Political Psychology, for her work on the psychology of victimhood. The role and function of completed and incomplete mourning are discussed as fundamental aspects of unresolved ethnic and sectarian conflict, and the importance of asking and granting forgiveness in the resolution of violent political conflict is explored. The paper ends with a specific application of the meaning of forgiveness in the relationship between Christians and Jews.

[[RunningHead]]

What Nursery School Teachers Ask Us About—Psychoanalytic Consultations in Preschools.

Edited by Erna Furman. Madison, Conn.: Int. Univ. Press, 1986, xvi + 247 pp., $32.00.

Elaine Heffner

Elaine Heffner

Elaine Heffner, Ed.D.
New York, NY

Elaine Heffner  

The transformation of psychoanalytic theory into principles of child rearing and education has long presented a seductive challenge to psychoanalysts, psychologists, and educators. Even more enticing than treating emotional illness is the prospect of facilitating the development of the "healthy" personality through the agency of parents and teachers. Anna Freud described the irresistible temptation to devise a "psychoanalytic education" that would prevent neurosis by eliminating those attitudes and actions identified in clinical work as pathogenic agents. To this end, theory and therapy early on were translated into prescriptions for parents and educators. Freud, himself, reproached the education of his day for concealing sexuality and failing to prepare young people for aggression. "In sending the young out into life with such a false psychological orientation, education is behaving as though one were to equip people starting on a Polar expedition with summer clothing and maps of the Italian Lakes" (Civilization and its Discontents, S.E., 21, p. 134n). Increasingly, the nursery school (if not the nursery) has been assigned the task of providing the appropriate clothing and charting the proper course for life's expedition.

In the years since the early attempts to provide a "psychoanalytic education," theories of child development have moved beyond the reconstruction of childhood from the analyses of adults to data drawn from direct observation of children. Unlike earlier, more simplistic educational approaches based on a then new awareness of libidinal phases of development, contemporary early childhood education seeks to facilitate the mastery of developmental tasks specific to stages of emotional, social, and cognitive growth.

217

The "Sissy Boy Syndrome" and the Development of Homosexuality

David Raphling M.D.

David Raphling

David L. Raphling, M.D.
Washington, DC

David Raphling  

Richard Green's book is the product of a 15-year prospective study of young boys whose gender-role behavior had been characterized as "feminine." It is an outgrowth of previous studies of transsexualism by Green and others which have demonstrated that adult transsexuals consistently exhibited cross-gender behaviors as children. A significant number of homosexual men also have been found to have histories of cross-gender behavior during childhood. These findings generated the major goals of this prospective study: to investigate persistent cross-gender behavior occurring in boys and to study its influence on their emerging sexuality.

Comments on Charles Brenner's "Personal Chronicle"

Jule Eisenbud M.D.

Jule Eisenbud

Jule Eisenbud, M.D.
4634 East 6th Avenue
Denver, CO 80220

Jule Eisenbud  

August 18, 1987

In his "Notes on Psychoanalysis by a Participant Observer" Brenner (1987) makes several points which are either highly dubious or plainly incorrect in regard to the psi hypothesis and its application to psychoanalytic data and concepts. Not only is his account of a case I describe in my 1955 paper "On the Use of The Psi Hypothesis in Psycho-analysis" somewhat at variance with the facts, but so also is his intimation that publications having to do with the use of the psi hypothesis in psychoanalysis have virtually disappeared since his "Facts, Coincidence, and the Psi Hypothesis" (1957) appeared. In regard to the former, I can only refer the reader to a careful reading of my paper referred to above, where I make it quite plain that it was only the warrant for the application of the psi hypothesis that was at issue, and that whether or not anything like psi actually was in the picture could not be decisively determined under the circumstances involved. In regard to the second point, Brenner arbitrarily limits his evidence for psychoanalytic interest in psi phenomena to journals, and to a highly personal view as to what constitutes a "reputable" psychoanalytic journal at that. Offhand, I can think of a handful of books having to do with psychoanalytic applications of the psi hypothesis that have appeared since his 1957 paper, including several of my own, as well as a handful of articles in the Psychoanalytic Review.

In any case, for Brenner to imply that a disappearance of publications on psi from the analytic literature had anything in particular to do with the effect of his "Facts, Coincidence, and the Psi Hypothesis" is grievously stretching chance coincidence, as well as gratuitously disregarding my comments on this paper in the same issue of the International Journal.

557

[[RunningHead]]

Practice and Precept in Psychoanalytic Technique: Selected Papers of Rudolph M. Loewenstein

Introduction by Jacob A. Arlow. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press, 1982, 240 pp., $22.50.

Stanley Goodman M.D.

Stanley Goodman

Stanley Goodman, M.D.
Berkeley, California

Stanley Goodman  

Whether or not one has already read each of Loewenstein's contributions to the literature on psychoanalytic technique, the opportunity to study and think about them as a collected body of work should reconfirm their power to stimulate and instruct analysts at every level of experience. Although the title of the volume suggests an emphasis on precepts, the approach of this master of theory, practice, and teaching would have been better conveyed by the title "Practice and Concept in Psychoanalytic Technique." The various analytic precepts, maxims, and rules referred to in the papers are unfailingly subjected to a searching review in the light of practical experience and current conceptual understanding. If indeed there is a single general precept or didactic injunction implied throughout the book it is the recommendation for continuing attention to the mutual validation of practice and concepts. Repeatedly, in the discussion of clinical examples or theoretical formulations, that attitude is demonstrated superbly by Loewenstein.

847

William W. Meissner, S.J.
Cambridge, Massachusetts

William Meissner  

This is a work of philosophical analysis that applies trenchant criticism to the philosophical bases of traditional Freudian theory, including not only Freud's early instinct theory, but the later developments of ego psychology as represented in the work of H. Hartmann, D. Rapaport and R. W. White. The criticism is thoughtful and forceful, clearly argued and articulated, and provides a powerful and fundamental reassessment of some basic psychoanalytic conceptions. While the author's thesis is clearly and forcefully argued, psychoanalytic readers will find it heavy going, and both challenging and thought-provoking. This is not a book to be read casually or offhandedly.

[[RunningHead]]

Track II Diplomacy

Harold Bronheim M.D.

Harold Bronheim

Harold E. Bronheim, M.D.
1155 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10028

Harold Bronheim  

March 13, 1988

I read with great interest Dr. Volkan's (1987) paper: "Psychological Concepts Useful in the Building of Political Foundations between Nations: Track II Diplomacy. It is seldom nowadays that one discovers a new and refreshing approach to long-standing social dilemmas, especially in the sphere of international diplomacy. Approaching nations as group-individuals and applying psychoanalytic conceptualizations to them not only explains heretofore irrational and complex behaviors in diplomacy and war, but also the possible means to stop regressions. I do wish to bring to your attention one additional issue.

It appears that Dr. Volkan tends to view adversaries as coequals who tend to magnify minor if not trivial differences. However, in individual psychology we have also delineated the difference between regressions and fixation. Despite periods of hostility when both nations may behave similarly and badly, it is important to remember that one nation may have seriously provoked the other nation into hostilities. We know for example that psychopaths and borderline individuals with poor impulse control, affective lability, proneness to regression, and object splitting are frequently provocative if not brutal. When confronted, they cry "foul" out of an extraordinary sense of entitlement. If hurt, they feel affirmed in their sense of victimization.

As psychoanalysts we know these individuals; and often it is only with the most painstaking self-examination that we prevent ourselves from retaliating within the therapeutic relationship. We occasionally fail. That we do does not however legitimize the patient's overweening expectations or destructive behavior. We have only temporarily regressed to the patient's level and have confirmed his infantile fears. We attempt, therefore, to protect patients, and the therapeutic alliance, from the outset with strict boundaries, and inform them of the consequences of destructive behavior, including rejection, from the relationship.

Leon Wurmser  

After slowly emerging from the ruins of dictatorship and world war, German psychoanalysis has developed into one of the largest psychoanalytic groupings, with an impressive number of institutes. It enjoys considerable support by universities and society, the latter especially in form of the broad financing of psychoanalytic treatment by the social insurance which covers almost the entire population. But it is not only a matter of quantity. A number of authors have dedicated themselves to a critical thinking-through of psychoanalytic theory and practice. In my opinion these reviews have assumed a depth and importance from which the psychoanalytic community at large can and should not close itself off. Under these reassessments the criticalempirical and epistemological works of Helmuth Thomä and Horst Kächele, the clinical reappraisals and philosophical contemplations of Wolfgang Loch, the editorial and encyclopedic work of F.-W. Eickhoff, and the many-sided, historically and culturally deeply founded essays of Johannes Cremerius may assume leading positions.

Robert Michels

Robert Michels  

Psychoanalysis means different things in different parts of the world and in different historical periods. This book was written in France in the 1980's, a response to a French intellectual dialogue that began in 1968 and flourished in the 1970's. To an American reader, it seems dated and largely irrelevant to what most Americans today consider to be psychoanalysis. For some on this side of the Atlantic it might illustrate that very special world of French intellectual life that is so difficult to translate, both culturally and linguistically.

The authors, distinguished psychoanalysts, start by arguing that psychoanalysis "is not an addition to the psychiatric arsenal of treatments" but "the key which unlocks the doors to knowledge of the species, in all aspects of our behavior and activities" (p. 30).

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Process, Theory, Clinical Observation and Empirical Research

By Joseph Weiss, Harold Sampson, and The Mount Zion Research Group. New York: Guilford Press, 1986, 423 pp., $49.95.

Susan Lazar M.D. , Ira Dosovitz M.D. and Robert Ursano M.D.

Robert Ursano

Susan Lazar, M.D.
Ira Dosovitz, M.D.
Robert J. Ursano, M.D.
Bethesda, MD

Robert Ursano  

The authors are to be commended for bringing empirical methodology to the study of psychoanalysis. They do the field a great service by operationalizing psychoanalytic concepts into competing hypotheses and examining the testable consequences of these different views of the mind. Weiss and Sampson are correct when they say, "There is no strong tradition within psychoanalysis of deducing testable consequences from a particular hypothesis … if psychoanalytic theory is to be tested empirically, such deductions are necessary." The authors rightly separate treatment from the research process and emphasize the necessity of replication.

Despite the title of their book, Weiss and Sampson's study is not only of the psychoanalytic process, but also of the much larger issue of mental functioning. They explore mental functioning, using psychoanalytic treatment as their microscope and experiemental method. Very few other studies so explicitly use psychoanalysis as a research tool, which in itself makes this work worthwhile.

The authors' research method involved verbatim transcripts and process notes of an analysis carried out in a different city by an analyst unfamiliar with the research hypotheses. Rater measures of the patient's and analyst's behaviors were made with an attempt to maintain rater blindness. Blindness is always a difficult process in this type of research since it is frequently difficult to decide what raters should be blind to.

Nearly all of the studies reported make use of the first 100 sessions of the analysis of Mrs. C. The one report on termination uses the last 100 sessions. One can rightly wonder whether the authors' hypotheses would be supported if a different phase of analysis were examined.

Negativism and Countertransference

Steven Levy M.D. and Lawrence Inderbitzin M.D.

Lawrence Inderbitzin

Steven T. Levy, M.D.
Lawrence B. Inderbitzin, M.D.
Emory University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research
1711 Uppergate Drive, N.E.
Atlantic, GA 30322

Lawrence Inderbitzin  

ABSTRACT

The purpose of this work is to explore the phenomenon of negativism and the analyst's response to it during the course of analytic work with a patient in whom negativism is a central behavioral pattern. Melville's short story, "Bartleby the Scrivener," describing in telling detail the response of a sympathetic lawyer to profound and pervasive negativism in his legal scribe, is discussed as a literary analogy to the analyst-analysand dyad. Aspects of the concept of negativism within psychoanalysis are discussed. The potential usefulness of understanding certain unexpected countertransference responses to pervasive negativism is explored, as this is a relatively neglected area of psychoanalytic technique. A case is presented describing the analysis of a patient whose character, like Bartleby's, is a mixture of profound negativism along with schizoid, obsessional, and masochistic elements.

506 Wyndmoor Avenue

Sterrett Mayson  

SACHS OPENED THE PANEL BY DESCRIBING the evolution of its title. In discussing the original title, "Sexuality and the Neuroses," Sachs and the panelists made the discovery that marked changes had occurred in their approach to the topic over the course of their professional careers. They then decided it would be worthwhile to describe, trace, and attempt to understand these changes. This turned out to be a difficult task indeed. In Sachs's opinion, each panelist was successful in providing a highly personal vision of his understanding of the relation between sexuality and the neuroses and of the changes that had occurred in this understanding over the years. Sachs noted that each paper showed the problems the panelist had struggled with as a young analyst, the solutions found at that time, and how the old solutions compared with the panelist's current understanding. The panelists' careers spanned a crucial period in the history of psychoanalysis. They were candidates in training at a time when ego psychology was the dominant theoretical framework. Since that time, they have had to confront and integrate radical new understandings of transference and countertransference as well as the development of new models of the mind. This historical perspective added significance to the panelists' papers.

Spruiell began by describing his furtive discovery of Freud as a fifteen-year-old high-school student. Reading Freud then stirred up intimations about his own mind which called for exploration. The question of how to know one's own mind led Spruiell on to other questions—how to know one's own body and how to know the body and mind of another.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, May 9, 1987.

Panelists: Robert M. Gardner, Barbara Rocah, Vann Spruiell; Discussant: Dale Boesky.

245 East 50th Street

Sara Vogel  

SCHARFMAN INTRODUCED THE PANEL with the statement that its task would be to discuss the vicissitudes of sexuality in relation to the developmental processes of early childhood, emphasizing normal development as well as deviations.

Noting that persisting doubts about concepts of sexuality in early childhood could be further resolved through an understanding of sexual development which systematically integrates historical views and new speculations and observations, Scharfman reminded the panelists that Freud, in his "Three Essays on the Theory of Sexuality," first connected childhood and sexuality and clearly elaborated the idea that sexuality was not limited to genitality and was only secondarily at the service of procreation. Freud viewed sexual aberrations as persistences into later life of earlier stages in sexual development, which occurred either because of hereditary and constitutional factors or due to the vicissitudes of early object relations. Scharfman stated that Friedman would discuss the former, while Galenson would discuss the latter.

Scharfman pointed out that two of the panelists, Stoller and Tyson, use the term "gender identity" in the titles of their presentations—a recent notion, but one which owes its derivation to the study of issues of identity formation which followed upon Freud's earlier formulations of ego psychology.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, May 9, 1987.

Panelists: Richard Friedman, Eleanor Galenson, Robert Stoller, Phyllis Tyson; Discussants: Samuel Ritvo, Sara A. Vogel.

Department of Psychiatry Robert Wood Johnson Medical School

Donald Spence  

July 25, 1988

In their critique of Narrative Truth and Historical Truth(Spence, 1982), Sass and Woolfolk (1988) appeal to the "hermeneutic claim that prereflective experience is itself patterned and meaningful" (p. 445). This claim parallels J. Lacan's famous assertion that the unconscious is structured like a language, and Freud's (1900) claim in Chapter 7 of The Interpretation of Dreams that the mechanisms of the primary process can account for the stuff of dreams. If infantile and/or prereflective experience does indeed contain a formal structure, then there is method in the psychoanalytic approach because transformation of these structures will appear in the patient's productions and we can reason from the latter to the former.

[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Karen J. Maroda

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
8 East 96th St.
New York, NY 10128

Martin Stein  

October 9, 1988

In her interesting letter, Dr. Maroda makes a number of statements which call for comment. First: "Most people are much better at talking about psychoanalysis than they are about doing it. Everyone knows this" (my italics). I assume "most people" applies to psychoanalytic practitioners. Is it true? And does "everyone" know it? I, for one, do not. I have listened to clinical reports for many years, as a supervisor of candidates in training and as a participant with colleagues in group discussions at CAPS (Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies) and at Aspen, and I find it difficult to support such a blanket statement.

Then: "… the average practitioner is often unsure of what a good intervention is. To some extent we are all winging it." It is difficult to be sure of what the "average practitioner" is sure of. And the term "winging it" presents a misleading idea of what we do most of the time. It gives the impression of loose guesswork, which is something we try at all costs to avoid. It is true that we work in a field of ambiguities and limited certainties. Our interventions often consist of tentative hypotheses, to be confirmed or disputed by subsequent events. We are, at our best, investigators, rather than prescribers, and we accept the inevitability of a number of false starts, for the sake of achieving a productive one. "Winging it" is hardly an adequate description of what we do most of the time.

Dr. Maroda makes much of the discouraging impact of the fear of being criticized for deviations from "classical analysis," being accused of not doing "real analysis" or of doing "wild analysis." While such criticisms are made, I have not heard them very often in the recent past. She glosses over the fact that some work is presented as psychoanalytic, which departs from the most fundamental principles of the field, e.g., interpretations based on little or no evidence, exhortation, warning, literal acceptance of surface phenomena without regard to unconscious elements, gross interference and manipulation of patients, etc. These may be therapeutically justified by extraordinary clinical emergencies, but they should not be thought of as psychoanalysis. And some work is simply bad, and should be regarded as such. As for the charge that those who deviate from "classical" analysis may fail to write or publish, this is certainly far from true of those with the courage of their convictions, from Jung to Kohut. That they should be immune to criticism, even harsh criticism, is too much to ask.

[[RunningHead]]

On Freud's "Project"

Irving Harrison M.D.

Irving Harrison

Irving B. Harrison, M.D.
10 Maplemoor Lane
White Plains, NY 10605

Irving Harrison  

August 12, 1988

I am writing to question the following statement, published in a Commentary (Schwartz, 1987): "The offspring of this union [of 'primitive neuroscience' with 'introspectively and empathically derived insights'], he named the Project for a Scientific Psychology (1895) … a model of the mind that Freud later viewed with shame intense enough to convince him that the work should not see publication during his lifetime" (p. 468).

It has always been my impression, based on biographic data about Freud and on the views of several of his close associates, that Freud recognized, with some chagrin, that the enormous effort he had made to create a scientific neuropsychology was incorrect, and that he decided not to publish it because he had come to realize that the only way to explain the mind was through the direct exploration of human feelings and thoughts, beginning with his own.

Can Dr. Schwartz document evidence either of Freud's alleged intense shame or of Freud's intention that the Project (which, incidentally, he did not name) be published after his death? If not, his statement is both incorrect and misleading, and is as well a gratuitous insult which is glaringly out of place in a psychoanalytic journal.

1130

[[RunningHead]]

Pseudostupidity and Analyzability

Lawrence Cohn M.D.

Lawrence Cohn

Lawrence S. Cohn, M.D.
8740 North Kendall Drive
Miami, FL 33176

Lawrence Cohn  

ABSTRACT

This paper seeks to heighten awareness of pseudostupidity and the potential analyzability of patients who manifest it by defining and explicating it, reviewing the literature, and presenting in detail the psychoanalytic treatment of a pseudostupid patient. Pseudostupidity is caused by an inhibition of the integration and synthesis of thoughts resulting in a discrepancy between intellectual capacity and apparent intellect. The patient's pseudostupidity was determined in part by his need to prevent his being more successful than father, i.e., defeating his oedipal rival. Knowing and learning were instinctualized. The patient libidinally and defensively identified with father's passive, masochistic position. He needed to frustrate the analyst as he had felt excited and frustrated by his parents' nudity and thwarted by his inhibitions. He wanted to cause the analyst to feel as helpless as he, the patient, felt. Countertransference frustration was relevant and clinically useful in the analysis. Interpretation of evolving relevant issues led to more anxiety and guilt, less pseudostupidity, a heightened alliance, and eventual working through. Negative therapeutic reactions followed the resolution of pseudostupidity.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Oedipus

By Peter L. Rudnytsky. New York: Columbia Univ. Press, 1987, 416 pp., $30.00.

George Nakari M.D.

George Nakari

George Nakari, M.D.
New York, NY

George Nakari  

Freud is reputed to have pointed to his bookshelf of Greek tragedies and proclaimed: "these were my teachers." The psychoanalyst's intellectual debts were of course more varied, and his teachers in general less grand than Sophocles and Aeschylus. However, despite Freud's varied intellectual origins, and his identification with numerous heroic figures from Joseph to Hannibal, it is Oedipus with whom we associate the father of psychoanalysis. And it is the identification of both Freud and his culture with the hero of Sophocles' play that Peter Rudnytsky's work, Freud and Oedipus, explores.

A beginning is the first step in the intentional production of meaning (Said, 1975, p. 5). Rudnytsky, Assistant Professor of Comparative Literature and English at Columbia University, has made the case for a new beginning from which the work of Freud can be situated and understood. Rudnytsky argues for a cultural "age of Oedipus" which found its apogee in Freud. The age of Oedipus, the author contends, began with the revolt against the melodramatic Latinist versions of the play (most notably Seneca's), that made Oedipus laughable to Voltaire and his Englightenment contemporaries. Gotthold Lessing and A. W. Schlegel, among others, are credited with fostering this return to Sophocles.

242

[[RunningHead]]

Spelunking as a Manifestation of a Counterclaustrophobia

Wayne Myers M.D.

Wayne Myers

Wayne A. Myers, M.D.
180 East 79th Street
New York, NY 10021

Wayne Myers  

ABSTRACT

Material is presented from the case history of a patient whose interest in spelunking (cave exploration) was found to be an unconscious expression of a type of counterclaustrophobia. Both oedipal and preoedipal determinants of the claustrophobic anxieties are delineated.

Of particular note in this instance is the testicular element in the genesis of the patient's claustrophobia. His confusion of the movements of his testicles into his inguinal canals during childhood defecation with the movements of the feces themselves lent an especial intensity to his fear of being flushed away from the mother by an expulsive anal birth from the claustrum. Childhood anxiety aroused when his testicles would become trapped in the inguinal canals was an important forerunner of the adult fear of being trapped in confined spaces.

A counterphobic element of the spelunking per se was his enjoyment in hanging suspended by a rope in caves. In this manner, he was able to act out (by virtue of his body-testicle equation) his identification with, and control over, the disappearing testicles in the setting of a claustrophilic union with the mother.

On Structures

Adrienne Applegarth M.D.

Adrienne Applegarth

Adrienne Applegarth, M.D.
1610 Scott Street
San Francisco, CA 94115

Adrienne Applegarth  

ABSTRACT

Some of the metapsychology involving the concept of structure is briefly reviewed, together with some difficulties presented by it. The concept of structure is defined, for purposes of the paper, and various kinds of structures are delineated. The author confines herself to the "microstructures," such as memories, cognitive structures, affect structures, defenses, identifications. Possible factors related to the development and maintenance of structures are discussed. In addition, the concept of structural change is taken up. It is suggested that an essential in such change is conscious review, however brief. The author proposes that structure formation and change is a fruitful area for mutual work by psychoanalysts and basic scientists.

Dream Frequency in Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Wayne Myers M.D. and Murray Solomon M.S.

Murray Solomon

Wayne A. Myers, M.D.
Murray Solomon, M.S.
180 East 79th Street
New York, NY 10021

Murray Solomon  

ABSTRACT

The authors examine data on dream frequency from 50 patients, half of whom were seen in psychoanalysis and half in psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Frequency, defined as the proportion of sessions where at least one dream is related, is analyzed statistically as a function of sex, age, and type of treatment. Also, treatment outcome is examined as a function of sex, type of treatment, diagnosis, and dream frequency. Psychoanalytic patients are found to have higher dream frequency than the patients in the psychotherapy group. Higher average dream frequency is correlated with a better treatment outcome in both patient groups.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Reconstructions and Empirical Data: Reciprocal Contributions

Peter Barglow M.D. , Charles Jaffe M.D. and Brian Vaughn Ph.D.

Brian Vaughn

Peter Barglow, M.D.
Charles M. Jaffe, M.D.
Brian Vaughn, Ph.D.
Department of Psychiatry Michael Reese Hospital
Lake Shore Drive at 31st Street
Chicago, IL 60616

Brian Vaughn  

ABSTRACT

Freud (1905), (1917), (1937) throughout his lifetime sought empirical scientific confirmation of the validity of his discoveries. In pursuit of this goal, he persistently emphasized the importance of establishing agreement between analytic reconstructions and the results of naturalistic child observation. The same objective lead Lichtenberg (1983), Emde (1981), (1985), and Stern (1985) to produce detailed evaluations of the impact of infant research findings on analytic developmental propositions. The present paper examines the relation among clinical reconstructions from an analysis developed through transference interpretations, empirical observations originating in the analytic patient's daughter's psychotherapy, and the results of empirical infant research that was being concurrently conducted by two of the authors.

The findings from the clinical analysis of the mother, the psychotherapy of the daughter, and empirical infant research all converged on the same larger causative factor for the daughter's psychopathology—a type of maternal deprivation. Such a confluence of different sources of evidence, each identified by a different method of investigation, provides one kind of validation for psychoanalytic reconstructions, making it possible to provide that "satisfactory degree of certainty" which Freud (1937) called for in the attempt to integrate the patient's "psychic truth" with "actual" or historical truth.

[[RunningHead]]

Dream Frequency and Treatment Outcome in Psychoanalysis and in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy

Wayne Myers M.D. and Murray Solomon M.S.

Murray Solomon

Wayne A. Myers, M.D.
Murray Solomon, M.S.
180 East 79th Street
New York, NY 10021

Murray Solomon  

July 18, 1988

As a followup to our earlier study (Myers and Solomon, 1989) on dream frequency in 25 psychoanalytic and 25 psychoanalytic psychotherapy patients, sensitivity and specificity analyses of the data were performed. Sensitivity is defined here as the probability of accurately predicting a good treatment outcome, while specificity refers to the probability of accurately predicting a poor or fair treatment outcome (see Mausner and Bahn, 1974).

We plotted the dream frequency levels (here defined as the percentage of sessions with at least one dream per session) against the sensitivity and specificity curves for the first 25, 50, 75, and 100 sessions for each of the 25 analytic and 25 analytic psychotherapy patients described in the original study. We chose these incremental cutoff points in order to see if there were any obvious differences between the findings at the varying points, thereby enabling us to say that outcome predictions based on dream frequencies for different numbers of sessions were either different or the same.

In the analytic patient group, the intersection of the specificity and sensitivity curves was at a point where we could predict a good and bad treatment outcome (by the criteria described in the earlier study) with 75% of accuracy, when there was at least one dream per session in 25% of the sessions (dream frequency of 25%). This was constant within the 25 session increments, thus allowing us to say that a dream frequency level of 6% at the end of 25 sessions was as good a predictor of outcome as one of 25% at the end of 100 sessions.

1123

[[RunningHead]]

Pains and Gains: A Study of Forced Terminations

Diane Martinez M.D.

Diane Martinez

Diane Martinez, M.D.
The University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio
7703 Flogd Curl Drive
San Antonio, TX 78284

Diane Martinez  

ABSTRACT

The forced termination of psychoanalysis, such as occurs when the analyst makes a geographic move, uniquely disrupts the analytic setting. This paper recounts the author's experience of terminating a full-time private practice of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic therapy for such a move. The limited literature on the subject is reviewed with a focus on the use of technical variation in the forced termination situation. The author delineates three areas of interaction with patients where technical variation proved in her experience to be of value: dealing with countertransference and counter-reaction, providing information about the move, and the consideration and process of referral for continued therapy. As opposed to what would be predicted from a classical psychoanalytic perspective, the use of such technical maneuvers seemed to facilitate rather than impede analytic work. These variations in technique served at crucial times to maintain the analytic alliance, to preserve the patient's capacity to recognize and make use of transference, and to provide avenues for resolving past traumas in the transference and the actual loss of the analyst. The concept of the analyst as a new or useable object is proposed as providing a theoretical framework for understanding these observations.

Panic Attacks Facilitating Recall and Mastery: Implications for Psychoanalytic Technique

Austin Silber M.D.

Austin Silber

Austin Silber, M.D.
1199 Park Arenue
New York, NY 10128

Austin Silber  

ABSTRACT

A patient, during the course of a lengthy analysis, experienced several panic attacks. These regressive episodes, occurring only in the analytic setting, provided her with her special means for recalling and eventually mastering repressed elements of her life. This facilitating aspect of her disorganization, when identified and analyzed as such, helped deepen the patient's analytic experience.

It was necessary for the analyst to define verbally and clarify her reconstructed past as it emerged as part of her panic state, transferentially, so that the patient could finally recognize, acknowledge, and understand hitherto hidden aspects of her life.

The psychiatric literature in regard to panic states is briefly noted, as is the relevance of comparing the present almost exclusively organic, pharmacological orientation, with Freud's discussion of the "actual" or current neuroses of his day, almost a century ago. On the basis of this case report, the importance of understanding the content and meaning of the panic attack, by analytic means, is once again emphasized.

The Patient-Analyst Match and the Outcome of Psychoanalysis: A Pilot Study

Judy Kantrowitz Ph.D. , Ann Katz , Deborah Greenman M.D. , Humphrey Morris M.D. , Frank Paolitto M.D. , Jerome Sashin M.D. and Leonard Solomon Ph.D.

Leonard Solomon

Judy L. Kantrowitz, Ph.D.
Ann L. Katz, Ed.D.
Deborah A. Greenman, M.D.
Humphrey Morris, M.D.
Frank Paolitto, M.D.
Jerome Sashin, M.D.
Leonard Solomon, Ph.D.
334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02146

Leonard Solomon  

ABSTRACT

This paper presents a pilot study in which we explore the possibility that the match between analyst and analysand is a factor of central importance in the analytic situation. It is an attempt to look at the issue of match across a large number of patients. The data we used were not collected for studying this topic; thus, the study has serious limitations. Nevertheless, certain patterns emerged which support our thesis that patient-analyst match plays a significant role in the outcome of psychoanalysis. We present this work as a first step in developing concepts and methods which will be pursued in a more systematic and rigorous fashion in a later study.

The Female Psychoanalyst in the Movies

Glen Gabbard M.D. and Krin Gabbard Ph.D.

Krin Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard, M.D.
Krin Gabbard, Ph.D.
The Menninger Clinic
Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601-0829

Krin Gabbard  

ABSTRACT

The last five decades of the American cinema have produced a remarkably consistent stereotype of the female analyst. In films such as Spellbound (1945), Knock on Wood (1954), Sex and the Single Girl (1964), They Might Be Giants (1971), and The Man Who Loved Women (1983), women analysts are swept away by countertransference love that leads them to become sexually or romantically involved with their male patients. In stark contrast to the data from studies of patient-therapist sexual involvement, there are more than twice as many films portraying unethical sexual behavior on the part of a female analyst as there are films depicting similar countertransference acting out by a male analyst. Moreover, a stable relationship with a man and a successful analytic practice never coexist for any woman analyst in the cinema. Neither does a female analyst ever successfully treat a male patient in the American cinema unless she falls in love with him. On the contrary, the male patient is more likely to cure his female analyst. Clinical reports from cross-gender analyses are useful in understanding these cinematic myths.

Aaron Esman  

ABSTRACT

To certain areas once dominated by psychoanalytic conceptualizations of psychopathology and pathogenesis, general psychiatry has in recent years made significant contributions. The obsessive-compulsive disorder is cited as an example, and illustrations of such contributions are described and discussed. Some implications for psychoanalytic theory and research are suggested.

How Dreams are Told: Secondary Revision—The Critic, the Editor, and the Plagiarist

Martin Stein M.D.

Martin Stein

Martin H. Stein, M.D.
8 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128

Martin Stein  

ABSTRACT

Secondary revision is a highly provocative concept arising out of Freud's attempts to explain the construction of dreams, but it remains relatively ill-defined. It includes three related, yet by no means identical aspects of the process by which the dream acquires its more or less final form during the experiencing, the remembering, and the telling. It represents one of the most interesting hypotheses dealing with the fluid world between sleeping and waking, a field which still presents us with a host of unanswered questions.

Secondary revision not only reflects the higher levels of the dreamer's mental functioning superimposed on his biological substructure, but it also operates as a sensitive indicator of the cultural factors which have helped mold his personality. These factors include both the subculture of the analytic situation and the impact of society in the larger sense. I make reference to individual dreamers in analysis and to the world of dreams recorded in the past from our own and other cultures.

Evaluation of Outcome of Psychoanalytic Treatment: Should Followup by the Analyst be Part of the Post-Termination Phase of Analytic Treatment?

Joseph Schachter M.D. and Morton Johan M.D.

Morton Johan

Joseph Schachter, M.D.
Morton Johan, M.D.
3708 Fifth Avenue
Pittsburgh, PA 15213

Morton Johan  

IN HIS INTRODUCTION TO THE PANEL, Schachter explained that his interest in the topic derived largely from a pilot study of outcome in which he had participated in Pittsburgh some years before. In his interviews with one of the two former patients, his impression of the gains the patient had made and those the patient had failed to make at the time of the followup was strikingly different from that of the treating analyst at the time of termination, five years before. This difference in assessment seemed due to the unique perspective provided by the passage of time, rather than to the fact that the evaluation was made by someone other than the treating analyst. It suggested to him that followup contact by the analyst would provide the treating analyst with a more valid picture of the results of his analytic work than was possible at the time of the termination. This would facilitate the analyst learning from his experience and would improve his ability to work with subsequent patients using the model of Freud in his discussion of the treatment of Dora. Schachter referred to a former panel on Reanalysis (this Journal, 33:187–200) which tended to confirm Freud's impressions in Analysis Terminable and Interminable, that all issues could not be accomplished in the first analysis because certain life events (i.e., pregnancy) had not brought them into the arena of the analytic consultation room. Schachter reviewed the sparse literature on post-termination followup, citing a 1950 report in the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. In that paper, four analysts advocated post-termination followup.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December 19, 1987.

Panelists: Lester Luborsky, Gary C. Martin, Nathan Schlessinger, Robert S. Wallerstein; Discussant: Kenneth T. Calder.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on "Writing about Psychoanalysis"

Karen Maroda Ph.D.

Karen Maroda

Karen J. Maroda, Ph.D.
Suite 601
1442 North Farwell Avenue
Milwaukee, WI 53202

Karen Maroda  

July 26, 1988

As a psychoanalytic psychologist, struggling daily with the prospect of detailing my clinical experiences and theoretical notions, I was pleased to see Stein's (1988a), (1988b) series on writing about psychoanalysis. I heartily agree with his assessment that most analytic writing is not

564

very good. To acknowledge that most writing in other scientific fields is not very good, either, is small consolation.

Stein's suggestions regarding more extensive and careful use of clinical vignettes and more candid and detailed accounts of therapeutic exchanges are useful ones. The question remains, if analytic clinicians produce extensive case studies reminiscent of Dora and the Wolf Man, will we then be able to reach the heights of insight and compassion that Freud did?

Stein seems to believe that we could, but I feel that he omitted some of the most important reasons why people either do not write well or do not write at all. First of all, most people are much better at talking about psychoanalysis than they are at doing it. Everyone knows this. Yet for obvious reasons, it is infrequently stated. Knowing how it should be done and doing it are entirely different propositions. Perhaps this is why most people avoid giving process notes, audio tapes, or any clear evidence of exactly what goes on in their sessions. Clearly, it is much easier and less threatening to talk about clinical syndromes or theory than it is to actually let other people in on what we really do when we are working. This also accounts for why there is so little information published on technique. You cannot speak coherently and productively about technique without giving very specific examples of precise therapeutic interventions.

The outcome of all this ambiguity is that the average practitioner is often unsure of what a good intervention is. To some extent we are all winging it, and we know it. The doubts and questions that naturally arise from attempting to implement such an individualized and ill-defined endeavor as analytic treatment are hidden, because many people assume they are the only ones who are not absolutely confident in what they are doing. The end result of this is that clinicians are afraid of being exposed not only as less-than-perfect, but as less-than-average. Under these circumstances, who is going to be willing to write about what actually goes on in their offices? Better to achieve recognition through a safe, interesting, and useful vignette, following the compulsory review of the literature.

[[RunningHead]]

A Phylogenetic Fantasy: Overview of the Transference Neuroses

By Sigmund Freud, edited by Ilse Grubrich-Simitis; translated by Axel Hoffer and Peter T. Hoffer. Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard Univ. Press, 1987, xvii + 113 pp., $17.50.

William Grossman M.D.

William Grossman

William I. Grossman, M.D.
Tenafly, New Jersey

William Grossman  

The discovery of the manuscript of one of Freud's lost metapsychological papers has understandably excited a great deal of scholarly interest. Although it has little to say to modern clinicians, the publication of the 1915 draft of the twelfth metapsychological essay, "Overview of the Transference Neuroses," provides a useful critical edition of an important transitional work in the development of Freud's thought. Like other works Freud excluded from his collected writings (On Aphasia, 1891) or discarded (the Project), this paper contains a systematic presentation of ideas that were not discussed so directly again. This is true, at least, for the latter part of the paper, which consists of the kind of "daringly playful fantasy" Freud considered a part of the mechanism of scientific creativity. It is in this section that he presents his "phylogenetic fantasy" that gives the American edition its name.1 As a whole, the paper marks a turning point in Freud's thought. Like the other metapsychological papers, it updates and summarizes his theories to that time. In addition, as part of Freud's large and largely unpublished correspondence with Ferenczi, this paper fits into the context of an intense personal relationship, in the same way that the Project(1895) belonged to the relationship with Fliess. For this reason, the "Overview" and the editor's informative essay will be indispensable for anyone interested in examining Freud's life and creativity. On the other hand, since most of the correspondence is unavailable, efforts to interpret the personal significance of this paper for Freud will, at best, be severely compromised, and at worst, merely daring fantasies.

In her critical essay, discussing the biographical context, the work context, and the historicoscientific context in which the paper was written, the editor, Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, begins the enormous task of placing this work in perspective and offers some clues to the questions of why Freud might have written this paper and why he did not publish it. She is appropriately cautious in view of the limited information thus far available.

Gerald Adler M.D.

Gerald Adler

Gerald Adler, M.D.
Massachusetts General Hospital West End House
16 Blossom Street
Boston, MA 02114

Gerald Adler  

ABSTRACT

The treatment of borderline patients provides a setting in which the analyst can evaluate the clinical usefulness of Kohut's theories. The issues that arise in this treatment can also highlight aspects of Kohut's theories that are incomplete and require other theoretical models in order to understand the patient optimally.

[[RunningHead]]

The Traumatic Element in the Typical Dream of Feeling Embarrassed at Being Naked

Wayne Myers M.D.

Wayne Myers

Wayne A. Myers, M.D.
180 East 79th Street (Apt. 1D)
New York, NY 10021

Wayne Myers  

ABSTRACT

Material is presented from three cases, where analysis of repetitive dreams of feeling embarrassment at being partially or totally naked was an important feature of the treatment. The indifference by the other people in the dream to the dreamer's nakedness was initially linked to perceived transference slights at the hands of the analyst, and later to repeated episodes of actually being treated indifferently at the hands of the parents. This indifference was related to latency or adolescent attempts by the patients to gain love or attention from the parents by exhibitionistic means.

The stereotypical presentation of the manifest content of these dreams is seen as evidence for their underlying traumatic roots. Such dreams are likened to the typical examination dreams described by Freud, which have also been noted by others to have traumatic roots. This finding is consistent with my own work with certain repetitive manifest dream configurations and with Freud's (1920) reevaluation of his theory of dreams in Beyond the Pleasure Principle, wherein he noted that dreams of patients suffering from traumatic neurosis often manifestly repeated the traumatic situation in an attempt to master it retrospectively.

[[RunningHead]]

Fetishism in a Woman

David Raphling M.D.

David Raphling

David L. Raphling, M.D.
5225 Connecticut Avenue, N.W.
Washington, DC 20015

David Raphling  

ABSTRACT

Fetishism is generally considered to be a perversion occurring only in males. In this paper a clinical illustration of an adult woman with a fetishistic perversion is offered and considered within the context of the classic concept of fetishism originally described by Freud. Similarities and differences between male and female fetishism are discussed, with attention given to the greater complexity of the perversion as it occurs in a woman. The clinical example confirms the existence of fetishism in women and suggests that subtle forms of the perversion may go unnoticed and be more prevalent than previously realized.

[[RunningHead]]

Emptiness as Defense in Severe Regressive States

Lucy Lafarge M.D.

Lucy Lafarge

Lucy Lafarge, M.D.
239 Central Park West (Suite 1-BE)
New York, NY 10024

Lucy Lafarge  

ABSTRACT

This paper examines the empty states experienced by severely ill borderline patients. At times of stressful regression, these patients use complaints of emptiness to describe profound disturbances of affect, cognition, object relations, and bodily experience. Empty states may be seen as complex defensive configurations which protect a borderline level of psychic structure from the impact of aggressively charged object relations, and ward off further regression to states of fragmentation or fusion. Severely ill borderline patients consolidate an empty screen by means of a characteristic repertoire of primitive defenses consisting of various forms of projective identification, including bitriangulation and projective identification of psychic agencies, somatization, acting out, and specific alterations in cognition. The author describes the highly deviant organizations of the object world seen in empty states, and the complex and disturbing countertransferences which these states evoke.

Borderline Psychopathology and its Treatment

By Gerald Adler. New York: Aronson, 1985, 255 pp., $25.00.

Marvin Margolis M.D.

Marvin Margolis

Marvin Margolis, M.D.
Franklin, MI

Marvin Margolis  

Gerald Adler is a compassionate, thoughtful and dedicated psychoanalyst whose new book reflects the author's two decades of therapeutic experience working with borderline patients. He acknowledges the contributions of his long-time collaborator, Dan Buie, while taking full responsibility for the present exposition of these views.

Adler traces the etiology of borderline pathology to a developmental failure in the formation of stable "soothing-holding introjects" during the phases of separation-individuation in the second and third years of life. These introjects are unstable and subject to a regressive loss of function.

[[RunningHead]]

The Opening Phase of Psychoanalysis

Joseph Lichtenberg M.D. and Elizabeth Auchincloss M.D.

Elizabeth Auchincloss

Joseph D. Lichtenberg, M.D.
Elizabeth L. Auchincloss, M.D.
230 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Elizabeth Auchincloss  

After a brief introduction by Lichtenberg, Malin opened the Panel with a review of the literature on the opening phase. He emphasized the need to consider two factors: first, that the rules and principles of psychoanalytic technique are being applied for the first time, and second, that there are theoretical considerations specific to the opening phase. Freud's Papers On Technique (1911-1915) deal with both these issues. In these papers, we find specific instructions as to the frequency and length of sessions, the use of the couch, the placement of the analyst, and the practice of taking notes; we also find more general principles such as the rule of abstinence, the fundamental rule of free association, and the analyst's use of evenly suspended attention. Freud presented some of his recommendations in three famous metaphors about technique: the metaphor of the surgeon, the metaphor of the analyst's unconscious as a receptive organ tuned to the transmitting unconscious of the patient, and the metaphor of the mirror. With regard to the establishment of a therapeutic relationship in the opening phase, Freud (1913) wrote that we need "an effective transference … in the patient, a proper rapport with him" before we can make interpretations. "It remains the first aim of the treatment to attach [the patient] to it and to the person of the doctor" (p. 139). Malin stressed that although Freud laid down rules which appear to be strict and severe, his suggestions were always accompanied by tempering remarks. For example, Freud (1912b) wrote, "… this technique is the only one suited to my individuality; I do not venture to deny that a physician quite differently constituted might find himself driven to adopt a different attitude to his patients and to the task before him" (p. 111). Later (1913) he wrote, "I think I am well advised … to call these rules 'recommendations' and not to claim any unconditional acceptance for them" (p. 123).

[[RunningHead]]

The Questions and Curiosity of the Psychoanalyst

Dale Boesky M.D.

Dale Boesky

Dale Boesky, M.D.
755 West Big Beaver Road (Suite 510)
Troy, MI 48084

Dale Boesky  

ABSTRACT

Questions the analyst asks of the patient are a powerful but neglected aspect of the theory of psychoanalytic technique. Their importance resides in their dynamic impact on the psychic equilibrium of the patient because of their ability to destabilize existing compromise formations. A clinical vignette illustrates the view that the linguistic shift from other modes of discourse to the spoken question heralds a less visible parallel shift in the psychic equilibrium of either the psychoanalyst or patient. There is no more and no less complexity to questions than to any other communication by either analyst or patient. For that reason there can be no systematic classification of questions in the clinical psychoanalytic situation, just as there can be no definitive taxonomy of psychoanalytic discourse. It makes no sense to reduce the enormous diversity and range of all the questions the analyst asks to the simple dichotomy of good or bad for the analysis. Questions are the emblem of the analyst's mode of inquiry; they can further the development of self-observation, which is such an important concomitant, cause, and consequence of structural change.

[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Negative Hallucination: Toward a Developmental Classification of Disturbances in Reality Awareness

Linda Brakel M.D.

Linda Brakel

Linda A. Wimer Brakel, M.D.
525 Third Street
Ann Arbor, MI 48103

Linda Brakel  

ABSTRACT

Negative hallucinations are explored from a psychodynamic viewpoint in four cases of neurosis. Next, shifting to the level of clinical theory, negative hallucinations are contrasted with five better-studied disturbances in reality awareness. It is noted that all six function as screen phenomena, and all occur in everyday contexts. These and other phenomenological issues lead to an assertion that negative hallucinations are regressive perceptual phenomena, similar in form to both preconscious perceptions and infantile amnesia. Finally, developmental considerations are used to derive a coherent hierarchy of disturbances in reality awareness.

[[RunningHead]]

Recent Contributions to the Theory of Defense Mechanisms: A Comparative View

Steven Cooper Ph.D.

Steven Cooper

Steven H. Cooper, Ph.D.
875 Massachusetts Avenue (Suite 54)
Cambridge, MA 02138

Steven Cooper  

ABSTRACT

Some of the most recent contributions to the theory of defense mechanisms are critically reviewed including theories of complex motivational properties of the ego (Schafer, 1968); (Kris, 1984), a functional theory of defense (Brenner, 1982), an object-representational theory of defense (Kernberg, 1976), a "two-person" theory of defense (Modell, 1984) and a self-psychologically based theory of defense (Kohut, 1984). These recently proposed theories of defense mechanisms utilize differing levels of analytic observation and theoretical discourse. One of the major differences among theorists involves the variety of referents of defense mechanisms (i.e., what is being defended against) including impulse, drive derivative, object loss, or environmental failure. Another fundamental difference involves the variety of ways theorists regard the relation between internal homeostasis and the external world. Questions are raised about the recent tendency in psychoanalytic theory to develop or invoke different theories of defense to explain a broad range of clinical phenomena.

[[RunningHead]]

On the Oceanic Experience

Irving Harrison M.D.

Irving Harrison

Irving B. Harrison, M.D.
10 Maplemoor Lane
White Plains, NY 10605

Irving Harrison  

February 11, 1988

In a recent essay, Werman (1986) expressed several opinions which contrast sharply with my own, published in the same year.

Werman (1986) regards "merging" as an experience unique to the oceanic feeling, and conducive to a "pantheistic world view" toward which, he states, the oceanic feeling directs one; he seems to endorse this as a goal in life stating that "the oceanic experience can play an incisive role in creative activity" (p. 136), that Freud's "regression model omits several crucial elements" (p. 37), and that Romain Rolland's "reality-focused achievements were actually enhanced by his oceanic experiences, which by their very nature brought Rolland into greater connection with humanity" (1986).

I advanced an opinion that the oceanic feeling is a totally unscientific concept, based on a belief in the possibility of one psyche merging with another. Rolland drew the term from Ramakrishna's account of a mystical experience associated with drowning, and claimed that rich beneficent energies can be tapped through willing immersion in it. His initial account to Freud, which fascinated him, invoked the religious feeling Rolland believed to be available to every individual. Rolland subsequently described deepening the experience through Yoga, which alerted Freud to Rolland's mystical preoccupations.

In endorsing Rolland's belief in the beneficial effects of the oceanic feeling, Werman has not differentiated clearly the oceanic feeling from that of awe. I also failed to emphasize that distinction (1966), (1979), (1986). Both share a subjective quality of feeling considered ineffable by the person affected. A number of transient conscious experiences may properly be regarded as related to one or the other; among these are a sense of mystical union, uncanny and eerie experiences, déjà phenomena, states of ecstacy or of wonder, fusion, merger, "seeing the salamander" (Plank, 1957) and "visits to eternity" (Weiss, 1986). Each of these terms has its own discrete nuance, but the fundamental difference among them is the distinction between the oceanic feeling and awe: a conviction about the existence of a source of mystical power is inherent in the former, and absent in the latter.

[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: Our Changing Science

Theodore Shapiro M.D.

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro  

Overheard

Psychiatrist A.: "An interesting schizphrenic patient. He loves cigars and when he lights one up the negative symptoms seem to disappear and he glows with pleasure."

Psychiatrist B.: "Perhaps cigar smoking has dopalytic activity.

Psychiatrist C.: (Passing by) "Sometimes a cigar is just a cigar.

Comment: "Some things never change."
—Sacks and Dixon (1989)

EXPLANATIONS AND DESCRIPTIONS, phenomenology and meaning, data and interpretation—each pair reminds us of the specific temporal flux in which we work—of how we look at what is "most real"—of how we grasp our world.

We have seen the rise of a Freudian interpretation of experience that emerged out of initial disbelief, and now we face another powerful shift in how we explain things. The sequence of comments in the epigraph to this brief note actually occurred in a modern hospital. It is amusing insofar as the allusion to a comment by Freud is clear, but something has happened to the explanation that suggests a change in what modern psychiatric training considers to be an adequate explanation of behavior. The comment marks the time of the encounter; it could not easily have occurred before 1985. The explanation in terms of neurotransmission clearly suggests that our younger colleagues are trying to apply what they learn about the substrate of the mind and to use it as an adequate explanation of behavior and mood. It has become commonplace to look for linkages across levels of observation and to hear phrases such as "chemical depression," genetic shyness, family aggregation of generalized anxiety disorders, panic attacks and agoraphobia, Clomipramine-sensitive obsessive-compulsive disorder (or more recently, fluoxitine-sensitive).

Changing Psychic Structure Through Treatment

Sander Abend M.D. and Edward Nersessian M.D.

Edward Nersessian

Sander Abend, M.D.
Edward Nersessian, M.D.
72 East 91st Street
New York, NY 10128

Edward Nersessian  

ABEND INTRODUCED THE PANEL by acknowledging the difficulties inherent in the panelists' task of first attempting to define psychic structure and then of determining what clinical signs they take as evidence of change in these structures. He noted that while the differences among the panelists were far-reaching and significant, there was agreement on at least two matters—first, that the transference is the vehicle through which these changes come about, and second, that behavioral or symptomatic change is not to be taken as one and the same with structural change, which implies a more thorough alteration in mental functioning. Reformulating the panel's task more precisely, he suggested that the panelists narrow their focus and discuss how they see the transference as a means for altering psychic structure, and what technical considerations, if any, they derive from their viewpoint.

The opening paper, "The Assessment of Structural Change as a Problem in Psychoanalytic Therapy (and Psychoanalytic Therapy Research)" was presented by Wallerstein. Beginning with a historical overview, Wallerstein cited D. Rapaport's conceptualization of structures as "controls and defenses whose rates of change are slow" as being seen by many as perhaps the most succinct and definitive in the psychoanalytic literature. In his 1960 monograph on The Structure of Psychoanalytic Theory, Rapaport contrasted these relatively abiding determiners of behavior and symptom with the drive processes, which he characterized as having a rapid rate of change and a paroxysmal course. J. Sandler and W. G.

[[RunningHead]]

Pitfalls of a One-Sided Image of Science: Adolf Grünbaum's Foundations of Psychoanalysis

Edwin Wallace M.D.

Edwin Wallace

Edwin R. Wallace, IV, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry and Health Behavior Medical College of Georgia
Augusta, GA 30912

Edwin Wallace  

ABSTRACT

Adolf Grünbaum's (1984) The Foundations of Psychoanalysis has received extensive attention in psychoanalytic and philosophical circles. Aspects of Grünbaum's argument are reviewed and criticized. While his volume is an important contribution to the epistemological assessment of psychoanalysis, it reflects serious shortcomings in at least four areas: its treatment of the role of suggestion in the analytic enterprise, its scrutiny of the psychoanalytic genetic method, its appreciation of analytic methodology as actually practiced, and, above all, its predication on a unidimensional, positivistic vision of science. Alternative approaches to the philosophy of psychoanalysis are suggested.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aliens Among Us: a Representation of Children in Science Fiction

Ilsa Bick M.D.

Ilsa Bick

Ilsa J. Bick, M.D.
8427 Upton Drive
San Antonio, TX 78250

Ilsa Bick  

ABSTRACT

As a literary genre, science fiction has been largely ignored by psychoanalysis. Science fiction lends itself well to analytic interpretations since its structure embraces an attitude of "cognitive estrangement" (a term that defines the genre). Science fiction allows for the exploration of new and different permutations of seemingly ageless conflicts and concerns. One of the conflicts science fiction seems to address revolves about our fears regarding our children. These children become the "aliens" among us, as they seek to usurp parental power and authority.

This issue is addressed through a study of two of Ray Bradbury's short stories. By manipulating the reader's experience of the "uncanny," Bradbury succeeds in tapping what appear to be prevalent and potent fears regarding children and, reflexively, the adults who produce them. Mechanisms involved in this play on "alienness" include projective identification of destructive aspects of the self, a resurgence of archaic superego forerunners constituted around primal scene material, and a reawakening of oedipal struggles.

responseSet.title: On a Type of Heterosexuality, and the Fluidity of Object Relations
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On a Type of Heterosexuality, and the Fluidity of Object Relations

David Hershey M.D.

David Hershey

David W. Hershey, M.D.
8226 Douglas (Suite 432)
Dallas TX 75225

David Hershey  

ABSTRACT

This paper delineates a form of heterosexuality whose underlying psychological structure resembles that of perversions. It is characterized by: idealization of instinctual processes, whereby the ego subordinates the object to the actualization of the instinctual event itself; heightened narcissism in an otherwise neurotic ego organization; multiple intrapsychic uses of the object; a special kind of defensive organization; an experienced victory over the superego and associated elevation of certain aspects of the ego ideal; and a resultant alteration in the nature of close interpersonal relationships.

I use clinical vignettes from both male and female patients in psychoanalysis to focus on the phenomena in question. The thinking of Chassequet-Smirgel, Freud, Khan, Rangell, Socarides, and others is used in an attempt to gain an encompassing perspective. It is emphasized that patients demonstrating this form of heterosexuality do not possess a perversion per se, but demonstrate a character neurosis which integrates certain perverse mechanisms into its defensive organization. These mechanisms can be isolated and studied in psychoanalytic treatment, with the potential for illuminating their defensive and mood-regulating functions, and eventual working through and resolution of the intrapsychic conflicts underlying them. Some comments on the implications of this form of sexuality for our contemporary culture are included.

Intolerance of Responsibility for Internal Conflict

Stanley Coen M.D.

Stanley Coen

Stanley J. Coen, M.D.
305 West End Avenue (Apt. 12B)
New York, NY 10023

Stanley Coen  

ABSTRACT

A type of patient is described, who has marked intolerance of taking responsibility for his internal conflicts so as to confront them, analyze them, and change. Defensive repetition in pathological object relations aims to avoid what is wrong within and to engage another so as to protect oneself. Genetic, dynamic, and technical aspects of such defensive, dependent relating are considered. Responsibility for oneself and for what is within oneself is held to be terrifying—more than anyone can bear on his own. How destructiveness has become, and remains, so terrifying is discussed.

Unconscious Fantasy

Theodore Shapiro M.D. and Lawrence Inderbitzin M.D.

Lawrence Inderbitzin

Theodore Shapiro, M.D.
Lawrence B. Inderbitzin, M.D.
The Emory University Psychoanalytic Center
1711 Uppergate Drive, NE
Atlanta, GA 30322

Lawrence Inderbitzin  

SHAPIRO OPENED THE PANEL by emphasizing that unconscious fantasy has always been a core concept in psychoanalysis. He traced its roots to the topographic theory, pointing up that the early theory permitted an adequate description of psychic representation and symbolized formation, but did not address the contradiction implied in cathectic mobility and stable unconscious structures that repeatedly sought emergence into consciousness.

Shapiro noted that J. A. Arlow and C. Brenner demonstrated the advantages of shifting from a noun concept of the unconscious to an adjectival form (descriptive unconscious). Fantasies were no longer classified with respect to presumed residence, but rather with respect to their representative stability in consciousness. Arlow and Brenner suggested that the topographic notion of the preconscious is superfluous, and emphasized how fantasies are represented and what ego functions are operative in the formation of compromise. Unconscious conflict resolutions in fantasy reside as anachronistic residues in the mental life of individuals, expressed in varieties of experiences explored in the analytic situation. Shapiro contrasted this classical view of unconscious fantasy with views of other theorists who have taken over the unconscious fantasy as a commodity for fair trade. Shapiro noted that our concept has been fostered away and neglected, abused, or renamed and developed so that we are no longer sure of its parentage.

Shapiro said that while the panelists may not present the last word on this topic, they were selected because they "all have something to say about this topic, are articulate, and … willing to meet each other without shame, animosity, or reluctance to discuss what is on [their] minds."
